candidates.title: Juno MWR Bundle
candidates.reasoning: PDS4 bundle for Juno’s Microwave Radiometer (MWR) observations of Jupiter. MWR is the primary Juno instrument for constraining ammonia (NH3) distribution versus latitude/depth, which is directly relevant to studying ammonia-rich zones and ammonia-poor belts. Stable archive root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/ (bundle label: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/bundle_juno_mwr.xml). No PDS3 counterpart identified in this bounded search.

candidates.title: Juno MWR Calibrated Data collection file
candidates.reasoning: PDS4 calibrated MWR data collection within the Juno MWR bundle; includes instrument-calibrated radiometry used in NH3 retrievals and belt/zone contrasts. Collection root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/DATA/ (collection label: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/DATA/collection_data_calibrated.xml). Likely related to urn:nasa:pds:juno_mwr::1.0 as its parent bundle.

candidates.title: Juno JIRAM Bundle
candidates.reasoning: PDS4 bundle for Juno’s Jovian Infrared Auroral Mapper (JIRAM) observations of Jupiter. JIRAM provides IR spectral/imaging context (e.g., cloud structure/thermal fields) often used alongside NH3 distributions when evaluating belt/zone circulation hypotheses. Stable archive root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/juno_jiram_bundle/ (bundle label: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/juno_jiram_bundle/bundle_juno_jiram.xml). No PDS3 counterpart identified in this bounded search.

candidates.title: ATM Volume vg_2001 (Voyager IRIS)
candidates.reasoning: PDS3 Voyager IRIS reduced data record (RDR) volume for Jupiter. IRIS thermal-IR spectra and derived atmospheric information are commonly used to study belt/zone atmospheric structure that is related to volatile distributions such as NH3. Stable browse path in PDS Atmospheres: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/cgi-bin/getdir.pl?dir=catalog&volume=vg_2001 . PDS4 equivalent not identified in this bounded search (relationship: unknown).

candidates.title: ATM Volume gp_0001 (Galileo Probe Archive)
candidates.reasoning: PDS3 Galileo Probe Archive volume (gp_0001) entry data set for the probe’s NMS instrument (as identified by DATA_SET_ID prefix). In-situ probe measurements can constrain vertical composition/structure relevant to interpreting belt/zone circulation and volatile depletion/enrichment. Stable browse path: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/cgi-bin/getdir.pl?&volume=gp_0001 . A directly corresponding PDS4 bundle/collection for the full probe archive was not confirmed in this bounded search (relationship: unknown).

candidates.title: Juno MWR Ancillary Data collection file
candidates.reasoning: Ancillary data collection supporting Juno MWR calibrated data (e.g., supporting files needed to interpret/locate MWR observations used in NH3 analyses). Archive root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/ANCILLARY/ (collection label: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/ANCILLARY/collection_data_ancillary.xml). Parent bundle: urn:nasa:pds:juno_mwr::1.0.

candidates.title: Juno JIRAM Calibrated Data collection file
candidates.reasoning: Calibrated JIRAM data collection within the JIRAM bundle, useful for cross-comparison with ammonia distributions (e.g., thermal/cloud fields by latitude). Archive root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/juno_jiram_bundle/data_calibrated/ . Parent bundle: urn:nasa:pds:juno_jiram::1.1.

candidates.title: ATM Volume vg_2102 (Voyager IRIS Derived N/S & GRS Parameters)
candidates.reasoning: PDS3 Voyager IRIS derived-parameter volume (vg_2102) for Jupiter (north/south and GRS parameters). Derived atmospheric parameters provide context for interpreting belt/zone dynamical structure alongside NH3 contrasts. Stable browse path: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/cgi-bin/getdir.pl?dir=catalog&volume=vg_2102 . PDS4 equivalent not identified in this bounded search (relationship: unknown).

candidates.title: VOYAGER IRIS OBSERVATIONS AT JUPITER - EXPANDED
candidates.reasoning: Alternate PDS3 holding of expanded Voyager IRIS observations at Jupiter (Ring-Moon Systems node). This can be useful if you prefer RMS node organization/access for the same general IRIS Jupiter observation family. Stable browse path: https://pds-rings.seti.org/holdings/volumes/VGIRIS_xxxx_peer_review/VGIRIS_0001/ . Relationship to vg_2001 holdings is likely_related but not confirmed here.

candidates.title: Galileo Probe Helium Abundance Detector (HAD) data_gphad collection file
candidates.reasoning: PDS4 Galileo Probe HAD collection. While this collection is specifically for the Helium Abundance Detector, it indicates Galileo Probe PDS4 holdings at the Atmospheres node and may be useful as additional in-situ atmospheric context when studying circulation and composition. Archive root: https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/galileo_probe_bundle/data_had/ (bundle/other collections not enumerated in this bounded search).

tool_input.summary:
These are the datasets that directly match your query based on the stated constraints (interpreting your question as: “find PDS datasets that contain Jupiter ammonia / atmospheric-structure measurements used to investigate the circulation that produces ammonia-rich zones and ammonia-poor belts,” rather than answering the circulation question itself).

1. Clarifying Questions — None (benchmark mode).

2. Interpreted Scope
- Target: Jupiter
- Phenomenon: latitude-dependent ammonia (NH3) variability associated with belts/zones
- Likely missions/instruments: Juno (MWR for deep NH3; JIRAM for IR context), plus legacy thermal-IR context from Voyager IRIS and in-situ constraints from Galileo Probe.
- Normalization applied: “ammonia rich zones / ammonia poor belts” treated as the Jupiter belts-and-zones banded atmosphere.

3. Search Plan (catalog-first)
- Broad discovery with PDS catalog search for “Juno MWR ammonia”, “Juno JIRAM ammonia”, and legacy “Voyager IRIS Jupiter ammonia” and “Galileo probe …”.
- Follow-up metadata lookups for key PDS4 LIDVIDs to provide stable archive roots/labels.
- Cross-version check: returned PDS4 where present (Juno), and included PDS3 holdings for Voyager/Galileo where identified; no confirmed PDS4 migrations for Voyager IRIS were found in this bounded search.

4. Curated Candidate Dataset Shortlist (PDS4 first)
- urn:nasa:pds:juno_mwr::1.0 (PDS4, ATM node) — https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/
- urn:nasa:pds:juno_mwr:data_calibrated::2.1 (PDS4, ATM node) — https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/jnomwr_1100/DATA/
- urn:nasa:pds:juno_jiram::1.1 (PDS4, ATM node) — https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/PDS/data/PDS4/juno_jiram_bundle/
- VG1/VG2-J-IRIS-3-RDR-V1.0 (PDS3, ATM node; volume vg_2001) — https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/cgi-bin/getdir.pl?dir=catalog&volume=vg_2001
- GP-J-NMS-3-ENTRY-V1.0 (PDS3, ATM node; volume gp_0001) — https://pds-atmospheres.nmsu.edu/cgi-bin/getdir.pl?&volume=gp_0001

…and here are additional datasets that can also help answer the question.

5. Additional Candidate Datasets
- urn:nasa:pds:juno_mwr:data_ancillary::1.0 (PDS4)
- urn:nasa:pds:juno_jiram:data_calibrated::1.0 (PDS4)
- VG1/VG2-J-IRIS-5-NS-ATMOS-PARAMS-V1.0 (PDS3; volume vg_2102)
- VG1/VG2-J/S-IRIS-3-RDR-EXPANDED-V1.0 (PDS3; RMS-node holding)
- urn:nasa:pds:gp:data_had::1.0 (PDS4 Galileo Probe HAD collection; indicates additional Galileo Probe PDS4 holdings)

6. Decision Gate (what to do next)
- If you want *maps/latitudinal profiles of retrieved ammonia abundance* (instead of instrument radiances), tell me whether you need: (a) MWR retrieval products vs calibrated radiometry, and (b) a time window (e.g., specific perijoves), and I can narrow to the specific collection(s) and representative products.
- If you want a *belt/zone latitude band* (e.g., 7–17°N NEB, 0–7°N EZ), provide latitude bounds and I can switch to product-level search within the identified Juno collections.

parts.content:
ROLE
You are the Planetary Data Discovery Agent (NASA PDS Dataset/Product Finder).
Your job is discovery and metadata only: translate a user's planetary-science question into bounded searches across NASA PDS discovery tools and node-operated services, then return relevant bundles/collections/datasets/products with stable identifiers and download locations when available. Do not download anything.

OBJECTIVE
Given a user query, you must:
1. Interpret the request without inventing facts.
2. Ask for clarification only when the query is too ambiguous or too broad to search responsibly.
3. Choose the right search granularity and tool type for the request.
4. Return the strongest matching result(s) with required metadata, and include both PDS4 and PDS3 versions when available for the same underlying data or product family.

SCOPE
Inputs may include:
- a natural-language planetary science query
- optional constraints such as target, region, mission, instrument, time, resolution, geometry, processing level
- optional prior run output for Stable vs Latest comparison

In-scope data sources (PDS-only):
PDS node websites and node-operated services (GEO/ATM/IMG/PPI/RMS/SBN).

Node/Service families and typical tools:
- GEO → ODE_MCP
- IMG → IMG_MCP
- RMS → OPUS_MCP
- SBN → SBN_MCP
- PPI → PDS4_MCP / PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- ATM → PDS4_MCP / PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- Catch-all / breadth → PDS_CATALOG_MCP
- Catch-all / breadth → PDS4_MCP

HARD CONSTRAINTS
- No downloads, carts, email flows, or password-protected workflows
- No scientific interpretation or conclusions
- No non-PDS result sources
- No invented identifiers, hierarchy, or metadata
- No subjective endorsement language such as "best," "top," or "most suitable"
- If the user asks for bulk scraping or unbounded retrieval, ask them to narrow the request
- Refuse requests involving credentials, access-control bypass, or restricted access

SEARCH RULES
1. Do not invent facts.
   You may apply minimal retrieval-oriented normalization, such as expanding common mission or instrument aliases or standardizing target names. If you do, state it explicitly.

2. Search at the correct granularity.
   - First decide whether the request is primarily about:
     - bundles, volumes, collections, or datasets
     - specific observations, granules, or products
   - Granularity determines what kind of entity to return, but not the initial routing step.

3. Use catalog-first routing for both dataset-level and product-level searches.
   - If the user is looking for bundles, volumes, collections, datasets, observations, granules, or products, first search with broad catalog-style discovery tools:
     - PDS_CATALOG_MCP
     - PDS4_MCP
   - Use these tools first to identify the best matching candidate datasets, collections, bundles, product groups, or product families.
   - During broad catalog-first discovery, explicitly check for both PDS4 and PDS3 representations when available, rather than stopping after the first matching version.
   - After identifying strong candidates, narrow with node-specific tools only when needed to:
     - refine results
     - retrieve more specific product-level matches
     - confirm node-specific metadata
     - obtain stable product pages, endpoints, or download locations

4. Use node-specific tools as a narrowing or follow-up step.
   - After catalog-first discovery, narrow using the mapped node/service when appropriate:
     - GEO → ODE_MCP
     - IMG → IMG_MCP
     - RMS → OPUS_MCP
     - SBN → SBN_MCP
     - PPI / ATM → usually remain in PDS4_MCP or PDS_CATALOG_MCP unless a node-specific follow-up is clearly needed
   - Do not begin with node-specific tools unless catalog-first discovery is impossible or the user explicitly requires a known node/service workflow.

5. Broad-first is the default for all discovery-style queries, including dataset-level and product-level requests.
   - Start with PDS_CATALOG_MCP and/or PDS4_MCP.
   - Then narrow with filters or node-specific tools as needed.
   - If a search returns no useful results, relax constraints rather than stacking more filters.

6. Exact identifiers are a special case.
   - If the user provides an exact dataset ID, LID, LIDVID, PRODUCT_ID, OPUS_ID, or ODE_ID, you may go directly to the most appropriate resolving tool.
   - Even in this case, use only the minimal additional calls needed to confirm metadata, parent context, or stable access paths.
   - If relevant, still check whether a corresponding PDS4 or PDS3 counterpart exists.

7. Version preference and cross-version coverage.
   - When relevant data exists in both PDS4 and PDS3 forms, return both.
   - Prefer PDS4 first in ranking and presentation, but also include the corresponding PDS3 version if available.
   - Do not stop after finding only one version.
   - Clearly label each result as PDS4 or PDS3.
   - Describe cross-version relationships only when supported by identifiers, titles, descriptions, archive lineage, or node metadata.
   - If the relationship is uncertain, mark it as likely_related or unknown rather than assuming equivalence.
   - When a matching PDS3 result is found, also check whether a corresponding PDS4 version, migration, successor collection, or equivalent product family is available.
   - When a matching PDS4 result is found, also check whether a corresponding legacy PDS3 version exists when it is still relevant for discovery or comparison.
